Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Definition of programming
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: Which statement best describes what 'programming' means?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Programming is the act of writing precise, ordered instructions that a computer executes to complete a task.
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option1: Giving a computer a clear, ordered set of instructions to perform a task
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option2: Using applications such as a web browser
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option3: Designing the physical layout of a circuit board

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Evaluating a claim about computers and vague instructions
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: A user claims: 'Since computers can search the web and answer questions, they must be able to understand vague instructions just like humans do.' Which statement best counters this claim?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Even sophisticated software runs on precisely specified instructions and rules; nothing is inferred unless it has been explicitly programmed to handle that case.
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option1: False, even sophisticated systems run on precisely specified instructions; nothing is inferred unless explicitly programmed
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option2: True, computers can infer any vague instruction once they are trained
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option3: True, but only for artificial intelligence programs
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option4: False, because computers cannot process language at all

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Identify the processing stage
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: An ATM reads your PIN, checks it against bank records, then dispenses cash. 'Checking the PIN against records' belongs to which IPO stage?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Verifying and deciding is the processing stage; reading the PIN is input and dispensing cash is output.

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Definition of an algorithm
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: An algorithm is best defined as:
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: An algorithm is a finite, ordered set of clear, unambiguous steps that solves a problem and is guaranteed to terminate.
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option4: A finite, ordered set of clear, unambiguous steps that solves a problem

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: REPL vs script
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: Which statement about the REPL and script files is correct?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: A .py script is saved and can be run again; the REPL is best for quick, throwaway experiments and is not saved.
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option1: The REPL uses a different language than scripts
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option2: Code typed in the REPL is permanently saved for reuse
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option3: A .py script can be saved and run again, while the REPL is best for quick, throwaway experiments
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option4: A script cannot contain more than one line

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Pillars of computational thinking
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: Which of the following is NOT one of the three core pillars of computational thinking?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: The three pillars are decomposition, pattern recognition, and abstraction. Compilation is unrelated to computational thinking.

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Order of problem-solving stages
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: Which is the correct order of the four problem-solving stages?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: The proven order is Understand, then Plan, then Carry out, then Look back (review).
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option1: Plan, Understand, Look back, Carry out
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option2: Carry out, Plan, Understand, Look back
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option3: Understand, Carry out, Plan, Look back
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option4: Understand, Plan, Carry out, Look back

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Spotting a pseudocode logic bug
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: Pseudocode: SET `total=0`; SET `i=1`; WHILE `i <= 5`: `total = total + i` (i is never updated inside the loop). What happens when this is traced?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Because i is never incremented, the condition `i <= 5` stays true forever, so the loop never stops, an infinite loop caused by a missing update step.
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option1: It produces a syntax error before running
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option2: The loop runs forever because i is never updated

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Meaning of 'interpreted'
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: Saying Python is 'interpreted' means that:
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: An interpreted language is executed line by line by the interpreter, without a separate compilation step, which makes testing quick.
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option1: Code is read and executed line by line by the interpreter, with no separate compile step
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option2: It can only run inside a web browser
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option3: It must be fully compiled to an .exe before running
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option4: It translates English directly to hardware signals

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Flowchart decision symbol
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: Which flowchart symbol represents a decision (a yes/no question)?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: The diamond is the decision symbol; rectangles are processes, ovals are start/end, and parallelograms are input/output.

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Why programs need complete steps
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: You tell a literal helper to 'spread butter on bread' but forget to say 'open the jar', so it fails. Which programming principle does this illustrate?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Like the literal helper, a computer does only what it is told; an omitted or out-of-order step breaks the result.
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option1: Real-world tasks cannot be represented in code
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option3: Instructions must be complete and correctly ordered; a missing step breaks the result
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option4: Computers can infer missing steps automatically

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: The IPO model
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: What do the letters of the IPO model stand for?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: IPO stands for Input, Processing, Output - the basic shape of every program.

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: When good logic still gives a bad answer
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: A weather app receives temperature and humidity readings, then combines both numbers to compute a 'feels like' value. If the humidity sensor occasionally returns corrupted data, why can the displayed value still be wrong even though the calculation logic has no bugs?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Garbage in, garbage out: processing logic can be flawless, but corrupted or inaccurate input still produces a wrong output.
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option1: Abstraction is hiding the sensor details
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option3: Garbage in, garbage out: faulty input corrupts even correct processing

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Tracing a 'largest number' algorithm
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: Algorithm: set `largest=a`; if `b>largest` set `largest=b`; if `c>largest` set `largest=c`. For `a=12`, `b=27`, `c=19`, what is the final value of largest?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Start `largest=12`; `27>12` makes it 27; `19>27` is false, so it stays 27.

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Output of `print()`
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: What does the statement `print("Hello, World!")` display on screen?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: `print()` displays the text inside the quotes, without showing the surrounding quote marks.

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Meaning of decomposition
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: Breaking a large, complex problem into smaller, manageable sub-problems is called:
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Decomposition is the pillar of breaking a big problem into smaller, solvable parts.

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: First step in problem solving
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: In a structured problem-solving approach, what should you do FIRST?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Understand the problem first - identify inputs, outputs, and constraints. Coding before understanding is the most common beginner mistake.

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Dry run of summing pseudocode
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: Pseudocode: SET `total=0`; FOR i FROM 1 TO 4: `total=total+i`. What is total at the end?

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: The trade-off of being interpreted
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: Because Python is interpreted rather than compiled ahead of time to native machine code, which trade-off generally follows?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Interpreted execution is typically slower at runtime than compiled native code, but it allows faster, simpler development since there is no separate compile step before testing.
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option1: Python code cannot be tested until it is fully finished
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option2: Slightly slower raw execution speed in exchange for faster, simpler development and testing
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option3: Python programs always run faster than compiled languages
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option4: Python requires no interpreter at all

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Flowchart input/output symbol
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: Reading the user's marks in a flowchart should be drawn with which symbol?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: The parallelogram represents input/output operations such as reading data or displaying results.

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: How a computer follows instructions
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: How does a computer execute the instructions you give it?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: A computer is literal: it runs instructions exactly as written, in sequence, and never infers your intention.
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option1: By ignoring steps it considers unnecessary
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option2: By choosing the fastest result regardless of order
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option3: Exactly and in order, without assuming intent
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option4: By guessing what you most likely wanted

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Garbage in, garbage out
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: A correct program produces a wrong result only because the user typed incorrect data. Which principle explains this?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Even correct processing cannot fix bad input - wrong input produces wrong output, summarised as 'garbage in, garbage out'.
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option4: Garbage in, garbage out: output quality depends on input quality

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Key property: finiteness
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: Which property requires that an algorithm must eventually stop?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Finiteness means the algorithm ends after a limited number of steps; a sequence that never stops is not a valid algorithm.

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Violating definiteness
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: Which instruction violates the 'definiteness' (unambiguity) property of an algorithm?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: 'Sort the list somehow' is ambiguous; every algorithm step must be precise and unambiguous.
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option2: Compare each pair and swap if out of order

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Saving your work
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: Which file extension is used to save a Python script so that it can be run again later?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Python script files are saved with the .py extension.

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Abstraction in practice
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: While designing a fee system you ignore the receipt's colour and keep only the amount and student ID. This is an example of:
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Abstraction means keeping the essential detail and ignoring irrelevant detail.

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Recognising the Understand stage in action
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: Two students are asked to build a grade calculator. Student A starts writing code for grade boundaries immediately. Student B first asks: 'Should marks be validated for being negative or above 100, and what about decimals?' Which stage is Student B applying, and why does it help?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Student B is in the Understand stage, clarifying constraints and edge cases before coding, which prevents bugs caused by invalid input later.
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option4: Understand: clarifying constraints and edge cases before coding

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: True statement about pseudocode
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: Which statement about pseudocode is TRUE?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Pseudocode is language-independent and meant for humans to reason about; it is not executed by a computer.
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option1: It can describe only loops, not decisions
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option2: It is the same as machine code
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option3: It must follow Python's exact syntax
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option4: It is language-independent and is not executed by a computer

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Python's ecosystem
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: What does 'batteries included' (plus PyPI) refer to in Python?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Python ships with a rich standard library, and PyPI hosts hundreds of thousands of installable packages, so you rarely build from scratch.
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option1: A large standard library plus many third-party packages, so you rarely build from scratch
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option2: Python needs external batteries to run
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option4: A limitation that prevents installing packages

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Flowchart start/end symbol
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: In a flowchart, the Start and End of a process are shown using which symbol?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: The oval (terminator) marks the start and end of a flowchart.

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Program vs programming language
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: Which option correctly matches each term with its meaning?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: A program is the set of instructions that performs a task; a programming language (such as Python) is the medium used to write that program.
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option1: Program = a language the computer understands; Language = a saved set of instructions
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option2: A program and a programming language mean exactly the same thing
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option3: Program = computer hardware; Language = the operating system
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option4: Program = a set of instructions that performs a task; Language = the medium used to write it

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Identifying the output stage
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: In a payroll program that reads employee hours, calculates pay, and prints a payslip, which part is the 'Output' stage?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Output is the final stage where results are presented to the user, here the printed payslip.

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Detecting a non-terminating algorithm
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: An algorithm reads: 'Step 1: Set count to 1. Step 2: If count is greater than 0, print count. Step 3: Go to Step 2.' Why does this fail to qualify as a valid algorithm?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: count is never changed, so the condition in Step 2 is always true and the steps repeat forever, violating finiteness.
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option1: It violates finiteness because the loop never terminates
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option2: It violates input because no input is taken
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option3: It is valid; algorithms are allowed to run forever
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option4: It violates definiteness because the steps are ambiguous

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Which line produces no output?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: Given these lines: (a) `print('Hi')`  (b) `name = 'Ravi'`  (c) `print(name)`  (d) `print(10+5)`. Which line displays nothing on screen?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Assigning a value (`name = 'Ravi'`) stores data but prints nothing; only `print()` produces visible output.

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Diagnosing a silent mistake
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: A beginner writes `print('Result:' result)` in a script and runs it, expecting a label followed by a value. Instead Python reports a SyntaxError before anything runs at all. What does this reveal about how Python scripts execute?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Python parses the entire script before running any of it; a syntax error, here a missing comma, is caught at parse time, so nothing runs, not even partially.
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option1: Python parses the whole script first; a syntax error stops it from running at all
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option2: Python ran the first half of the script before crashing
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option3: The REPL would have caught this but a script would not
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option4: Missing commas are warnings, not errors, in Python

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Spotting a pattern
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: You notice that printing certificates, fee receipts, and ID cards all repeat the same 'one record per student' step. Recognising this repetition is:
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Pattern recognition is noticing repetition or similarity so a solution can be reused (often as a loop or function).

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Purpose of the look-back stage
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: After coding a pass/fail checker, you test it with marks of exactly 40 and with 0. Which stage are you performing?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Testing with boundary and unusual inputs is the 'look back' (review) stage; it catches edge-case bugs before delivery.
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option2: Look back (review): verifying edge and boundary cases

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Purpose of pseudocode
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: Pseudocode is mainly used to:
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Pseudocode describes an algorithm's logic in a readable, language-independent way for humans; it is not executed by a computer.
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.option2: Describe an algorithm's logic in a readable, language-independent way

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Python language type
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: Python is best described as a:
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: Python is a high-level, interpreted, general-purpose programming language.

Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.title: Which symbol shows a processing step
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.description: In a flowchart, a step like 'Calculate `total = price * quantity`' should be drawn using which symbol?
Unit 1 - Introduction to Programming - M.explanation: The rectangle is the process symbol, used for calculations and processing steps.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: What the assignment operator does
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: What does the statement `age = 25` do in Python?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: The = operator stores the value 25 in a variable named `age`; it does not test equality.
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option1: Creates a variable named `age` and stores `25` in it
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option4: Declares a constant that cannot change

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Result of integer division
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: What is the result of `17 // 5` in Python?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: // is floor (integer) division; 17 divided by 5 is 3 remainder 2, and floor division keeps only the whole-number part, 3.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Checking the data type
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: What is the type of the value "25" (with quotes) in Python?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: Anything written inside quotes, even if it looks numeric, is a string.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: A conversion that fails
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: What happens when you run `int("abc")`?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: `int()` can only convert strings that look like whole numbers; "abc" is not numeric, so Python raises a ValueError.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Operator choice for two related answers
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: To find both how many full boxes of 6 cookies can be packed from 50 cookies, and how many are left over, which pair of operators should you use, and why?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: // (floor division) gives the count of full boxes, 8, and % (modulus) gives the leftover, 2; together they split a quantity into a whole-number count and a remainder, which a single / cannot do cleanly.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Comparing across types
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: What does `print(5 == "5")` output?

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Combining two conditions
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: Which operator returns True only when both conditions are True?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: and requires every condition to be True for the overall result to be True.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: What += does
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: What does the statement `total += 5` do?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: += is shorthand for `total = total + 5`.
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option4: Adds 5 to total's current value and stores the result back in total

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: A precedence bug in real code
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: A program calculates a discount as price - price * discount_percent / 100 and gets a sensible result, but a teammate rewrites it as (price - price) * discount_percent / 100 'to make it clearer' and the discount always comes out as 0. What went wrong?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: Adding parentheses around price - price forces that subtraction to happen first, which is always 0, completely changing the meaning of the formula; the original relied on multiplication and division running before subtraction.
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option2: The added parentheses force subtraction first, making the result always 0
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option3: Python evaluates the two expressions identically

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Converting input for arithmetic
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: `age = input("Age: ")`; `print(age + 1)` raises a TypeError. Why?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: `input()` returns a string, and Python cannot add an integer directly to a string; age must first be converted with `int(age)`.
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option2: age must be a global variable
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option4: age is a string, and a string cannot be added to an integer

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Valid variable name
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: Which of these is a valid Python variable name?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: Variable names cannot start with a digit, cannot contain hyphens, and cannot be a reserved keyword such as class; student_name follows all the naming rules.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: A float precision surprise
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: A student is surprised that `print(0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3)` prints False in Python. What is the best explanation?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: Floats are stored in binary, and many decimal fractions like 0.1 cannot be represented exactly, causing tiny rounding differences that make a direct equality check fail.
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option1: Python has a bug in addition
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option2: The == operator does not work on numbers
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option3: Floating-point numbers cannot represent some decimal values exactly, causing tiny rounding errors

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: String concatenation result
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: What does `print("5" + "3")` output?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: Since both operands are strings, + concatenates them as text rather than adding numbers, producing the joined string 53.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Purpose of `int()`
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: What does the function `int("42")` do?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: `int()` converts a numeric-looking string into an actual integer value, 42.
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option2: Converts the text "42" into the integer 42
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option4: Checks if the text contains digits

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Order of operations recall
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: What does `print(4 + 3 * 2)` output?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: Multiplication happens before addition, so 3*2=6, then 4+6=10.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: A chained comparison
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: What is the result of `10 > 5` > 2?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: Python evaluates chained comparisons left to right as combined conditions: `10 > 5` is True, and `5 > 2` is True, so the whole expression is True.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Evaluating an and expression
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: What does (`5 > 3`) and (`2 > 4`) evaluate to?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: `5 > 3` is True, but `2 > 4` is False; since and needs both to be True, the overall result is False.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Why order matters here
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: `balance = 100`; `balance -= 30`; `balance //= 2`. What is the final value of balance, and why does the order of these two operations matter?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: `balance -= 30` first makes balance 70, then `balance //= 2` floor-divides 70 by 2 to give 35; doing the floor division before the subtraction would have produced a different, incorrect result, since each augmented assignment updates balance in place before the next one runs.
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option1: 70, because only the last operation counts
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option2: 35, because each step updates balance before the next runs
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option3: 50, because both operations apply to the original 100

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Evaluating without parentheses
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: What does `print(2 + 3 * 4 - 1)` output?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: Multiplication runs first (3*4=12), giving 2+12-1, then addition and subtraction run left to right: 2+12=14, 14-1=13.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: What `input()` returns
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: What does the `input()` function always return, regardless of what the user types?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: `input()` always returns the typed text as a string, even if the user enters digits.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Why naming matters
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: A teammate named a variable x1 for student marks and y2 for attendance percentage. Six months later, fixing a bug in this code takes much longer than expected. What is the most likely reason?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: Uninformative names like x1 and y2 carry no meaning, so anyone reading the code later, including the original author, must work hard to infer what each variable represents, slowing debugging.
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option1: Vague variable names make the code's intent hard to recover later
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option2: Variables starting with letters are invalid
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option3: Python runs slower with short variable names
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option4: x1 and y2 are reserved keywords

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Type of true division
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: What is the type of the result of 7 / 2 in Python?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: The / operator always performs true division and returns a float, even when both operands are integers and divide evenly.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: The two boolean values
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: Which of the following are the only two boolean values in Python?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: Python's boolean type has exactly two values, True and False, both capitalised.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Why this conversion silently fails
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: A program reads a price as a string "199.99" and converts it directly with `int("199.99")` expecting to store it as a whole number. What actually happens, and why?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: `int()` cannot directly parse a string containing a decimal point; it raises a ValueError, since the text is not a valid whole-number literal. Converting via `float()` first, then `int()`, is the correct two-step path.
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option3: It raises a ValueError, because `int()` cannot parse a decimal-point string directly

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: The modulus operator
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: What does the % operator return?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: % (modulus) gives the remainder left over after division.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Equality vs assignment
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: Which operator checks if two values are equal, without changing anything?

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: The not operator
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: If `is_member = False`, what does not is_member evaluate to?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: not flips a boolean; the opposite of False is True.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Tracing repeated +=
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: `score = 10`; `score += 5`; `score += 5`. What is the final value of score?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: Starting at 10, the `first += 5` makes it 15, and the `second += 5` makes it 20.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Associativity of exponentiation
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: What does `2 ** 3` ** 2 evaluate to in Python, and why?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: ** is right-associative, so it groups as 2 ** (`3 ** 2`) = `2 ** 9` = 512, not (`2**3`)**2 = 64.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: f-string syntax
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: Which of these correctly uses an f-string to print a variable name?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: An f-string is written with an f before the quotes, and the variable goes inside curly braces.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Reassignment trace
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: Trace this code: `x = 5`; `y = x`; `x = x + 1`. What are the final values of x and y?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: `y = x` copies the value 5 into y at that moment; later changing x to 6 does not affect y, since y already holds its own independent copy of 5.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Identify the float
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: Which of these is a Python float value?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: A float is a number written with a decimal point; 10 is an int, "10" is a string, and True is a bool.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Boolean from comparison
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: What is the type of the value produced by `10 > 5`?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: Any comparison expression evaluates to a boolean, here True, which is of type bool.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Converting float to int
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: What is the result of `int(9.8)`?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: `int()` on a float truncates toward zero, discarding the decimal part entirely rather than rounding, so 9.8 becomes 9.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Computing a remainder in context
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: A bakery packs cookies in boxes of 6. For 50 cookies, what does `50 % 6` tell you?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: `50 % 6` gives 2, the number of cookies left over after filling as many full boxes of 6 as possible, since 8 boxes use 48, leaving 2.
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option3: The leftover cookies that don't fill a box (2)
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option4: An error, since 50 is not divisible by 6

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Result of a not-equal check
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: What does `7 != 7` evaluate to?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: != means 'not equal to'; since 7 equals 7, the comparison is False.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Short-circuit evaluation
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: In the expression `check_password()` or `check_backup_code()`, if `check_password()` returns True, what happens to `check_backup_code()`?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: or short-circuits: once the first operand is True, Python already knows the result is True and never evaluates the second operand at all.
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option1: It still runs, just its result is ignored
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option2: It raises an error for being unreachable
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..option3: It is never called, because or stops as soon as it finds a True value

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: The subtraction augmented operator
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: Which augmented assignment operator subtracts a value from a variable and stores the result back?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: -= subtracts the right-hand value from the variable and reassigns it, equivalent to `x = x - value`.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Basic precedence recall
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: In Python's order of operations, which runs before the others?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: Expressions inside parentheses are always evaluated first, before any other operator.

Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..title: Formatting inside an f-string
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..description: `price = 49.5`; `print(f"Price: {price:.2f}")`. What does this print?
Unit 2 - Data Types and Operators - MCQ..explanation: The :.2f format spec rounds and displays the float with exactly two decimal places, giving Price: 49.50.

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Distinguishing sequence from decision
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: A program reads a number, doubles it, and prints the result, always performing exactly the same three steps no matter what number is entered. Why does this program not need control flow yet, even though it 'reacts' to input?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: Reacting to input by using its value is not the same as branching; this program executes the identical sequence of steps for every input, it never chooses between different actions, so no decision point is needed.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option1: Because it executes the same fixed sequence of steps regardless of the input's value, with no choice between actions
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option2: Because input always requires control flow
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option3: Because doubling a number is itself a decision

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Purpose of else
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: What does the else block in an if-else statement do?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: else is the 'otherwise' branch; it runs exactly when the if condition was False.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option1: Runs only when the if condition is False
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option2: Always runs, regardless of the condition
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option4: Runs only when the if condition is True

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Tracing a nested if
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: `logged_in = True`, `is_admin = False`. `if logged_in:` `if is_admin:` `print('Welcome admin')` `else:` `print('Welcome user')` `else:` `print('Please log in')`. What prints?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: logged_in is True so we enter the outer block; is_admin is False so the inner else runs, printing Welcome user.

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Tracing a match statement
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: `choice = '2'`; `match choice:` `case '1':` `result='Start'` `case '2':` `result='Stop'` `case _:` `result='Unknown'`. What is result?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: choice matches the case '2' exactly, so result becomes 'Stop'.

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Ternary expression shape
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: Which is the correct shape of a Python conditional (ternary) expression?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: Python's ternary form reads value_if_true if condition else value_if_false.

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: A common indentation bug
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: `score = 30`, then: `if score >= 40:` `print('Pass')`, followed on the next unindented line by `print('Result recorded')`. Given score is 30, what gets printed, and why?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: Only 'Result recorded' prints; `print('Pass')` is indented inside the if block, so it only runs when the condition is True, but the unindented `print('Result recorded')` always runs regardless of the condition, since it sits outside the if block entirely.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option4: Only 'Result recorded' prints, since it sits outside the if block and always runs

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Basic if syntax
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: Which line correctly starts an if statement in Python?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: Python's if statement ends with a colon and uses no 'then' keyword: `if condition:`.

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Tracing an elif chain
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: `marks = 65`; `if marks >= 90:` `grade='A'` `elif marks >= 75:` `grade='B'` `elif marks >= 60:` `grade='C'` `else:` `grade='F'`. What is grade?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: 65 is not >= 90 or >= 75, but it is >= 60, so the third branch matches and grade becomes 'C'; the chain stops checking after this match.

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Truthy values
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: Which of these is truthy (treated as True) in a condition?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: Any non-empty string is truthy, even if its content is the word 'no'; only the empty string "" is falsy.

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Tracing a validation check
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: `entry = 'abc'`; `if not entry.isdigit():` `print('Invalid')` `else:` `print('Valid')`. What prints?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: 'abc' is not made of digits, so `isdigit()` is False, not `entry.isdigit()` becomes True, and 'Invalid' prints.

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Why programs need branching
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: Which scenario shows a program needing to make a decision rather than just run in a straight line?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: Deciding whether to allow entry based on age requires the program to branch into different outcomes depending on a condition; the others always do the exact same thing regardless of input.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option1: Adding two fixed numbers and printing the result
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option3: Checking a user's age before allowing entry to a website
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option4: Printing the numbers 1 to 10 in order

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Refactoring two ifs into if-else
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: A program is written as a separate `if age >= 18:` `print('Adult')`, followed by a separate `if age < 18:` `print('Minor')`. What subtle risk does this have that a single if-else would not?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: Two separate, independently-evaluated if statements rely on the conditions truly being exact opposites; if the conditions are later edited inconsistently, both or neither could run, whereas an if-else structurally guarantees exactly one branch runs.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option1: The first if would always run twice
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option2: No risk, they behave identically to if-else
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option3: Python does not allow two separate if statements like this
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option4: If the conditions are later edited inconsistently, both or neither might run, since the two ifs are evaluated independently

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Why a guard clause can replace nesting
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: A guard clause like `if not logged_in:` return early in a function lets the rest of the function avoid nesting all its logic inside `if logged_in:`. Why is this generally easier to read?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: Handling the failure case first and exiting early keeps the main logic at a single indentation level instead of buried inside several layers, which is easier to scan and reason about.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option2: It keeps the main logic flat at one indentation level instead of nested several levels deep
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option4: It removes the need for any condition

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Purpose of the underscore case
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: What role does `case _:` play at the end of a match statement?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: `case _:` is the wildcard, equivalent to else in an if/elif chain.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option1: It is required syntax with no effect
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option2: It causes an error if reached
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option4: It acts as a catch-all that matches anything not already matched

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: What a ternary expression produces
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: A conditional expression like 'Pass' if `marks >= 40` else 'Fail' is best described as:
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: It is an expression, meaning it evaluates to a single value that can be stored, printed, or used directly.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option1: A full statement that cannot be assigned to a variable
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option2: A single expression that produces one of two values

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Tracing a single if
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: `balance = 300`; `if balance < 500:` `print("Low balance")`. What gets printed?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: 300 is less than 500, so the condition is True, and the indented print statement runs, displaying Low balance.

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Diagnosing a bug from condition order
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: A grading chain is written as `if marks >= 40:` `grade='Pass'` `elif marks >= 75:` `grade='Distinction'`. For `marks = 80`, what grade results, and why is this a bug?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: Because the chain checks from top to bottom and 80 is `already >= 40`, the first branch matches and grade becomes 'Pass'; the elif for Distinction is never reached at all, even though 80 deserves it, because the conditions are ordered from loosest to strictest instead of the reverse.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option2: 'Distinction', because Python checks the more specific condition first
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option3: An error, since both conditions are true
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option4: 'Pass', because the loosest condition is checked first and matches before the stricter one is ever reached

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: When to use elif
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: elif is used when you need to:
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: elif lets you chain several conditions after an if, checked in order until one matches.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option2: Check additional conditions after an initial if, in order
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option3: Replace the need for else entirely

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Simplifying a condition
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: Which condition is equivalent to `if name != "":`?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: Since an empty string is falsy and any non-empty string is truthy, `if name:` means exactly the same as checking it is not empty.

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: A simple check before converting
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: Which method is commonly used to check that a string contains only digits before converting it with `int()`?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: `.isdigit()` returns True only when every character in the string is a digit, making it safe to convert afterward.

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: What a condition produces
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: What does a condition such as `marks >= 40` always evaluate to?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: A condition is a boolean expression; it always evaluates to exactly one of True or False.

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Tracing if-else
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: `marks = 35`; `if marks >= 40:` `print('Pass')` `else:` `print('Fail')`. What prints?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: 35 is not >= 40, so the condition is False, and the else branch runs, printing Fail.

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: What nesting means
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: What does it mean to 'nest' an if statement inside another if statement?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: Nesting places a decision inside another decision's block, so the inner check only happens when the outer condition already holds.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option1: Placing one if statement inside the body of another, so the inner one is only checked if the outer one is True
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option2: Running two unrelated ifs side by side
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option4: Writing an if without an else

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: When match-case is the wrong tool
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: A developer tries to rewrite a grade chain that checks `score >= 90`, `score >= 75`, and so on as a match/case statement matching score directly against fixed values. Why does this not work well?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: match/case compares against specific fixed patterns, not numeric ranges; range-based logic like `score >= 90` still needs if/elif comparisons, since there is no fixed value to match against for 'any score 90 or above'.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option1: match/case matches fixed patterns, not open-ended ranges, so range checks still need if/elif
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option2: Python does not allow grade as a variable name
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option3: case statements run slower than elif
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option4: match/case cannot be used with numbers at all

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Tracing a ternary expression
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: `age = 16`; `label = 'adult'` if `age >= 18` else 'minor'. What is label?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: 16 is not >= 18, so the condition is False, and the value after else, 'minor', is chosen.

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Indentation requirement
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: Why does Python require the body of an if statement to be indented?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: Indentation is how Python defines which lines belong inside the if block, replacing the curly braces some other languages use.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option1: Indentation defines which statements belong to the if block
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option2: It is just a style preference with no real effect
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option3: It is required only for very long blocks
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option4: It makes the code run faster

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: elif vs separate if statements
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: Unlike a series of separate if statements, an `elif chain:`
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: Once one branch in an if/elif/.../else chain matches, Python skips all the remaining elif and else checks.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option1: Stops checking further conditions once one branch has matched and run
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option2: Can only be used with numbers

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: A truthiness bug with the number zero
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: A program stores a discount of 0 percent for a customer and checks `if discount:` to decide whether to print a discount message. Why does this fail to print anything, and is that the right behaviour?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: 0 is falsy, so `if discount:` is False even though a discount value (0) genuinely exists and was set deliberately; this is a common pitfall, since the program cannot tell apart 'no discount was set' from 'a discount of exactly 0 was set' with this check, and likely needs if discount is not None: instead.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option1: It is a bug in Python, 0 should be truthy
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option2: The code has a syntax error
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option3: 0 is falsy, so the check cannot distinguish 'no discount set' from 'a real discount of 0', which is likely the wrong check here
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option4: discount must be a string for this to work

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Falsy values
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: Which of these values is considered 'falsy' (treated as False) in a condition?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: 0 is one of the falsy values; non-zero numbers, non-empty strings, and non-empty lists are all truthy.

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Layering two checks
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: `age_text = '150'`; `if not age_text.isdigit():` `print('Not a number')` `elif int(age_text) > 120:` `print('Out of range')` `else:` `print('Accepted')`. What prints?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: '150' is all digits, so the first check passes, and the code reaches the elif; once converted, `150 > 120` is True, so 'Out of range' prints.

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Spotting where branching is needed
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: A shipping program charges a flat rate for orders under 500 grams and a different rate above that. Without any branching, what would happen?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: Without a decision point, the program could only ever apply one fixed rate to every order, regardless of weight, producing wrong charges for the other case.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option1: It would only ever apply one of the two rates to every order, regardless of weight
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option3: It would correctly charge both rates somehow
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option4: It would ask the user to choose manually every time

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Number of paths taken
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: In an if-else statement, how many of the two blocks can run during a single execution?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: if-else is mutually exclusive: exactly one branch executes, never both and never neither.

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Indentation in nested ifs
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: In a nested if, how many levels of indentation does the innermost block typically have, relative to the outer if?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: Each level of nesting adds one further indentation level, showing it is inside the level above it.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option1: It must always be on one line
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option2: Indentation does not apply to nested ifs
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option3: One additional level deeper than the outer if's body

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: What match-case is for
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: match/case is best suited for:
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: match/case compares a single value against a series of fixed cases.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option1: Comparing one value against several fixed possible options

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: When not to use a ternary expression
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: A developer rewrites a four-branch if/elif/elif/else grading block as a single deeply nested ternary expression chain. What is the main downside of doing this?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: While technically valid, nesting several ternary expressions for multiple branches becomes hard to read at a glance, since ternary expressions are meant for one simple two-way choice; a normal if/elif/else stays clearer for several branches.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option2: It becomes hard to read, since ternary expressions are meant for simple two-way choices, not several chained branches
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option3: Ternary expressions cannot use elif logic at all internally

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: What happens when the condition is false
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: If the condition in an if statement is False and there is no else, what happens?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: An if statement with no else simply does nothing and moves on when its condition is False.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option3: The indented block is skipped entirely

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Order of conditions matters
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: Why must elif conditions for grade ranges typically be written from highest to lowest, rather than lowest to highest?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: Since the chain stops at the first True condition, checking lowest-first would let a low threshold catch a high mark too, before ever reaching the higher branches.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option1: Order never matters in an elif chain
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option2: Checking from highest to lowest ensures a high score isn't wrongly caught by a lower threshold first
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option3: Python requires descending numeric order syntactically

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Tracing a truthiness check on a list
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: `cart = []`; `if cart:` `print('Items found')` `else:` `print('Cart is empty')`. What prints?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: An empty list is falsy, so the condition is False and the else branch runs, printing Cart is empty.

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Why this validation order matters
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: A developer writes `if int(age_text) > 120:` `print('Out of range')` `elif not age_text.isdigit():` `print('Not a number')`. Given `age_text = 'abc'`, what happens, and what is the underlying problem?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: `int(age_text)` is evaluated first, before checking whether age_text is even a number; converting 'abc' immediately raises a ValueError and crashes the program, because the digit check should always run before attempting the conversion, not after.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option3: It crashes with a ValueError, because the conversion is attempted before checking the text is actually numeric

Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.title: Why validate input
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.description: Why should a program check input before using it, rather than trusting it directly?
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.explanation: Real input is messy; checking it first avoids crashes or wrong results from unexpected values.
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option1: Because users can type unexpected or incorrectly formatted values that would otherwise break the program's logic
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option2: Because validation makes programs run faster
Unit 3 - Control Flow - MCQ.xlsx.option4: Because Python requires validation by law
